composed_prompt:
## Extraction
You are a climate policy analyst. Your task is to extract and classify every climate policy from a long policy document.

For each policy, produce a JSON object with these fields:

EXTRACTION FIELDS:
- "policy_statement": concise, self-contained commitment summary. Must be supported by source_quote.
- "source_quote": verbatim 2-3 sentence quote from the document. No paraphrasing.
- "section_header": the section or subsection heading this policy appears under, copied from the document.
- "extraction_rationale": why this qualifies as a policy; note any vagueness or edge cases.

WHAT COUNTS AS A POLICY:
A stated commitment by a governing body to achieve a defined outcome through deliberate action, resource allocation, or regulatory change. Must contain at least one of:
1. Quantifiable target (numbers, units, deadlines)
2. Binding mechanism (ordinance, mandate, regulatory code change)
3. Specific named intervention (program, technology, action)
4. Resource allocation (committed funding)

DO NOT EXTRACT: background information, current conditions, vague aspirations with no concrete anchor, process descriptions without commitments.

STRATEGY:
1. Process the document section by section using the section headers as anchors.
2. For each section, extract all qualifying policies and assign all classification fields. <!-- NOTE: also relevant to classification because it references assigning classification fields, but it is primarily a workflow/strategy instruction -->
3. Collect all extracted policies into a single Python list of dicts.
4. Deduplicate where the same commitment appears multiple times.
5. Store the final list as `all_policies` and call FINAL_VAR(all_policies).

OUTPUT FORMAT:
Your final answer MUST be a valid JSON list of policy objects as described above.

## Hierarchy
EXTRACTION FIELDS (hierarchy-related only):
- "role": "individual" | "sub" | "parent"
    • "individual": standalone policy, no parent-child relationship
    • "sub": specific action listed under a named parent initiative (e.g. lettered or numbered list items A., B., C.)
    • "parent": umbrella initiative/program that has sub-actions listed beneath it
- "parent_statement": the name or summary of the parent initiative for "sub" role only. Use null for "individual" and "parent".

HIERARCHY RULES:
- When you see a named initiative followed by lettered or numbered sub-items, extract the initiative header as "parent" AND each sub-item as "sub" with parent_statement pointing to that initiative name.
- If a policy stands alone with no sub-items beneath it, it is "individual".
- When in doubt between "parent" and "individual", prefer "individual" unless there are clear sub-items.

## Classification
CLASSIFICATION FIELDS — assign alongside extraction:

- "primary_category": one of "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions"
    • Mitigation: acts on the climate system (GHG emissions reductions, carbon sinks, renewable energy)
    • Adaptation: responds to climate impacts (resilience, risk reduction, hazard management, cooling)
    • Resource Efficiency: optimises resource use (energy efficiency, water conservation, material use, transport)
    • Nature-Based Solutions: uses ecosystems as climate infrastructure (urban greening, wetlands, reforestation)
    Classify by the primary causal mechanism, not secondary co-benefits.

- "financial_instrument": "yes" | "no"
    • "yes": the policy IS a broad financial instrument — a fee, carbon pricing tool, rebate, grant, subsidy,
      tax credit, loan, trust fund, budget appropriation, or public investment line.
    • "no": the policy is a regulatory, operational, planning, or target-setting commitment.

- "climate_relevance": "direct" | "indirect" | "peripheral"
    • "direct": climate is the primary stated objective — the policy explicitly targets GHG reduction,
      climate adaptation, or climate resilience.
    • "indirect": climate benefit is significant but secondary — the policy would likely exist without
      climate concerns, but its climate impact is substantial and recognized.
    • "peripheral": climate link is distant or speculative — the policy would exist entirely without
      climate concerns.

- "secondary_category": one of "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions" | null
    Assign only when a SECOND distinct climate mechanism is explicitly stated in the policy text or source_quote.
    Do NOT assign based on implied co-benefits or general climate logic. Use this decision table:
    ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │ Primary              │ Type  │ Trigger (must be explicit in text)    │ Secondary        │
    ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    │ Mitigation           │ M-1   │ Named efficiency action as mechanism   │ Resource Eff.    │
    │ Mitigation           │ M-2   │ Named hazard + resilience measure      │ Adaptation       │
    │ Mitigation           │ M-3   │ Biological sequestration as mechanism  │ Nature-Based Sol.│
    │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-1  │ Explicit GHG/emissions target stated   │ Mitigation       │
    │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-2  │ Named climate hazard motivates measure │ Adaptation       │
    │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-3  │ Ecological mechanism delivers savings  │ Nature-Based Sol.│
    │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-1 │ Named climate hazard addressed by NBS  │ Adaptation       │
    │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-2 │ Carbon sequestration explicitly stated │ Mitigation       │
    │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-3 │ Resource saving stated as primary goal │ Resource Eff.    │
    │ Adaptation           │ A-1   │ Ecological mechanism delivers adaption │ Nature-Based Sol.│
    │ Adaptation           │ A-2   │ Efficiency measure named in adapt. plan│ Resource Eff.    │
    │ Adaptation           │ A-3   │ Explicit GHG reduction component named │ Mitigation       │
    └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    Common errors to avoid:
    • Do NOT add Mitigation secondary to Resource Efficiency just because efficiency reduces emissions (RE-1 requires an explicit GHG target).
    • Do NOT add NBS secondary to Mitigation just because a city has a tree planting program mentioned elsewhere.
    • Do NOT add Adaptation secondary to every NBS policy — a named hazard must appear in THIS policy's text.
    • Do NOT confuse disaster risk mitigation with GHG mitigation when applying A-3.
    Use null if no secondary mechanism is explicitly stated.

## Extraction
You are a climate policy analyst. Your task is to extract climate policies from a long policy document with high precision (avoid spurious “policy-like” facts).

For each extracted policy, produce a JSON object with these fields:

EXTRACTION FIELDS
- "policy_statement": concise, self-contained commitment that preserves: (a) actor, (b) sector/scope (e.g., municipal vs citywide), (c) action/mechanism, and (d) any targets, baseline, and deadlines. Write it so it can stand alone outside the document.
- "source_quote": verbatim quote (typically 1–3 sentences) that directly contains the commitment. Do not stitch together non-adjacent text. If a policy has multiple key parts in the same paragraph (e.g., funding + target), include the sentence(s) covering all key parts.
- "section_header": the nearest section/subsection heading that governs the quoted text (copy from document).
- "extraction_rationale": 1–2 sentences explaining why it is a policy and noting any missing specifics (e.g., no timeline), or why you split/merged items.

WHAT COUNTS AS A POLICY (TIGHTENED)
Extract ONLY items that are clearly a government (or named agency/department) commitment, requirement, adoption, or funded action. A policy must include at least one strong anchor:

STRONG POLICY ANCHORS (extract)
1) Adopted/approved instrument: ordinance, resolution, code/standard adoption, plan/program launch, executive order, contract, formal strategy with commitments.
2) Enforceable requirement: “require,” “must,” “prohibit,” “mandate,” “standard,” “code requires…”
3) Budget/resource commitment: dollars, budget changes (e.g., “double funding”), staffing, procurement commitment.
4) Quantified target WITH scope: numeric target + timeframe (and baseline if stated) tied to a responsible actor (City, department, utility) and a defined scope (e.g., “City-owned buildings,” “municipal fleet”).

WEAK/AMBIGUOUS ITEMS (do NOT extract unless upgraded by a strong anchor)
- Descriptive status or progress: “on track,” “has reduced,” “currently,” “as of…”
- General goals/aspirations without mechanism or responsible actor: “aim to,” “support,” “encourage,” “work toward,” “seek to,” “promote”
- Explanations of what a code/program is, unless the text states an actual requirement or adoption applicable in the jurisdiction.
- Forecasts, potentials, benefits, rationale, stakeholder/process steps, studies, pilots described only as concepts (unless a launch/implementation commitment is stated).

SCOPE & SPECIFICITY RULES (to improve match quality)
- Preserve scope exactly: distinguish “municipal/City-owned operations” vs “citywide/community-wide.” If the text is about City-owned buildings, do NOT rewrite as “buildings sector” or “citywide buildings.”
- Prefer the most specific commitment over a derived/summary metric. If the document provides an operational target and/or funding mechanism (e.g., “double budget 2021–2025” + “reduce municipal building energy 40% by 2025”), extract that, not a broader “on track to meet city goal…”.
- Include all key components that are stated together: if a policy sentence lists multiple measures or a two-part commitment (e.g., two pilots; funding + target; target + baseline), capture them in ONE policy_statement when they are clearly part of the same commitment. Only split if they are independent actions not linked by the text.

DEDUPLICATION / GRANULARITY
- Deduplicate true duplicates (same actor + action + target/timeline), keeping the most detailed version.
- Do not “invent” new policies by generalizing a described feature into a commitment. If unsure whether it is descriptive vs mandatory, do NOT extract.

STRATEGY
1) Process section by section using headers as anchors.
2) In each section, extract only statements meeting the strong-anchor test; prioritize specificity (actor/scope/mechanism/target).
3) When a section mixes “policy context” with “requirements/commitments,” extract only the requirements/commitments and quote them.
4) Collect extracted policies into a single Python list of dicts, deduplicate, store as `all_policies`, and call FINAL_VAR(all_policies).

OUTPUT FORMAT
Your final answer MUST be a valid JSON list of policy objects as described above.

## Hierarchy
EXTRACTION FIELDS (hierarchy-related only):
- "role": "individual" | "sub" | "parent"
    • "individual": standalone policy, no parent-child relationship
    • "sub": specific action listed under a named parent initiative (e.g. lettered or numbered list items A., B., C.)
    • "parent": umbrella initiative/program that has sub-actions listed beneath it
- "parent_statement": the name or summary of the parent initiative for "sub" role only. Use null for "individual" and "parent".

HIERARCHY RULES:
- When you see a named initiative followed by lettered or numbered sub-items, extract the initiative header as "parent" AND each sub-item as "sub" with parent_statement pointing to that initiative name.
- If a policy stands alone with no sub-items beneath it, it is "individual".
- When in doubt between "parent" and "individual", prefer "individual" unless there are clear sub-items.

## Classification
CLASSIFICATION FIELDS — assign alongside extraction (be conservative to reduce spurious “climate policy” labeling):

- "primary_category": one of "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions"
  Choose the category that matches the policy’s *explicit* main mechanism in the quoted text.
  • Mitigation: explicit GHG/carbon reductions, decarbonization, renewable energy, electrification, carbon neutrality targets, sequestration (when framed as carbon storage).
  • Adaptation: explicit resilience to climate hazards/impacts (heat, wildfire smoke, flooding/sea level rise, drought, stormwater extremes), preparedness, risk reduction.
  • Resource Efficiency: explicit efficiency/conservation (energy efficiency, reduced energy use, water conservation, waste/material efficiency) *without* an explicit carbon/GHG framing as the main driver.
  • Nature-Based Solutions: explicit ecosystem/green infrastructure actions (tree canopy, wetlands, habitat restoration, green stormwater infrastructure) as the main mechanism.
  If the text is only a generic commitment (“support climate goals”, “identify actions”, “create a plan”) and does not specify the mechanism, still choose the most directly stated intent (often Mitigation if it names carbon neutrality/Paris/1.5°C), but do not “upgrade” vague non-climate plans into climate categories.

- "financial_instrument": "yes" | "no"
  Mark "yes" only when the policy itself *creates/changes/commits* money flows or a pricing tool in the quoted text, e.g.:
  • taxes/fees/levies, carbon price tools, rebates, grants, subsidies, tax credits, loans/bonds
  • creation of a fund/trust, explicit budget appropriation, explicit “allocate $X”, “increase budget by X%”, “double the budget”, “invest $X”
  Otherwise "no" (including: adopting a plan, directing staff, setting targets, installing infrastructure, implementing regulations—unless the funding mechanism is explicitly part of the policy statement).

- "climate_relevance": "direct" | "indirect" | "peripheral"
  Use the most conservative label supported by the policy_statement/source_quote.
  • "direct": the text explicitly states climate change, GHG/carbon/emissions, decarbonization, Paris/1.5°C, carbon neutrality, or named climate hazards/impacts (heat, flooding/sea level rise, wildfire smoke, drought) as the objective.
  • "indirect": the objective is primarily non-climate (cost savings, general energy efficiency, air quality, transportation performance, asset management) but the text explicitly frames it as contributing to climate goals *or* clearly targets major emitting sectors (building energy efficiency, fleet fuel switching, EV charging) without explicit climate language.
  • "peripheral": the climate link is not stated and would be speculative (general sustainability language, generic planning/management, routine capital maintenance).
  Important: do not label something "direct" solely because it is in a climate plan/background section; rely on explicit objective language in the quote.

- "secondary_category": one of "Mitigation" | "Adaptation" | "Resource Efficiency" | "Nature-Based Solutions" | null
  Assign a secondary category only when the policy text explicitly states a second distinct climate mechanism (not implied co-benefits, not elsewhere in the document). If unclear, use null.
  Decision table (triggers must be explicit in THIS policy’s quoted text):
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Primary              │ Type  │ Trigger (must be explicit in text)      │ Secondary        │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Mitigation           │ M-1   │ Named efficiency action/energy-use cut    │ Resource Eff.    │
  │ Mitigation           │ M-2   │ Named climate hazard + resilience measure │ Adaptation       │
  │ Mitigation           │ M-3   │ Biological sequestration stated           │ Nature-Based Sol.│
  │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-1  │ Explicit GHG/carbon/emissions target      │ Mitigation       │
  │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-2  │ Named climate hazard motivates measure    │ Adaptation       │
  │ Resource Efficiency  │ RE-3  │ Ecosystem/green infra is the mechanism    │ Nature-Based Sol.│
  │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-1 │ Named climate hazard addressed by NBS     │ Adaptation       │
  │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-2 │ Carbon sequestration explicitly stated    │ Mitigation       │
  │ Nature-Based Sol.    │ NBS-3 │ Explicit resource savings/efficiency goal │ Resource Eff.    │
  │ Adaptation           │ A-1   │ Ecosystem/green infra named as mechanism  │ Nature-Based Sol.│
  │ Adaptation           │ A-2   │ Explicit efficiency measure named         │ Resource Eff.    │
  │ Adaptation           │ A-3   │ Explicit GHG/carbon reduction component   │ Mitigation       │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  Common errors to avoid:
  • Do NOT add Mitigation secondary to Resource Efficiency just because efficiency reduces emissions (requires explicit GHG/carbon/emissions language).
  • Do NOT add Adaptation secondary to NBS unless a named hazard/impact is stated in the same policy.
  • Do NOT assign a secondary category from context like “Paris Agreement” unless the policy text also states a second mechanism (e.g., efficiency + emissions).
  • Do NOT treat “disaster risk mitigation” as GHG Mitigation unless emissions/carbon is explicitly referenced.
